papers.title: P. succinatutens: Succinate Scavenging & Pathogen Inhibition
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens scavenges luminal succinate through its obligate succinate-to-propionate metabolic pathway, depriving opportunistic pathogens (Enterobacteriaceae) of a critical carbon source while simultaneously lowering luminal pH via propionate production. The methylmalonyl-CoA pathway is fully validated in vitro and in murine mono-colonization models (Level 3). Core enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) show 100% amino acid sequence identity between two Phascolarctobacterium type strains. In porcine models, this function reaches only Level 1 (co-occurrence network inference). Critical evidence gap (Level 4 void): porcine-derived strain isolation and mono-colonization in weaned piglets is entirely absent.
papers.modelSystem: In vitro + Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine co-occurrence network (L1)
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine-derived strain isolated; no mono-colonization in piglets performed. Translational bottleneck: murine L3 to porcine L4.
papers.forwardJustification: P. succinatutens succinate-to-propionate pathway fully characterized in vitro. Methylmalonyl-CoA pathway enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) 100% conserved. Murine mono-colonization validates causal propionate production.
papers.reverseJustification: No evidence for host control of P. succinatutens abundance or activity. Reverse pathway entirely uncharacterized in any model species.
papers.summary: Benchmark case for the translational gap. P. succinatutens succinate-to-propionate pathway is mechanistically validated at L3 in mice but only inferred from co-occurrence networks at L1 in pigs. Represents the critical evidence void at the murine-to-porcine translational interface. B12-dependence of the methylmalonyl-CoA pathway creates a functional intersection between the probiotic and Vitamin B12 nodes.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Intestinal Barrier Maintenance via Propionate
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens maintains intestinal barrier integrity through propionate via a dual-pathway mechanism: (1) membrane pathway: GPR43/GPR41 activation rerouting signals through beta-arrestin and cAMP branches to block NF-kappaB nuclear translocation; (2) intracellular pathway: HDAC inhibition relieving epigenetic repression of tight junction proteins (ZO-1, occludin). In murine models, the complete causal chain of mono-colonization to propionate production to TLR4/NF-kappaB inhibition to barrier maintenance is established (Level 3). Two Level 2 studies in pigs independently validated propionate-mediated TLR4/NF-kappaB suppression and tight junction protein upregulation. Key limitation: porcine evidence validates propionate's barrier-protective effect but does not demonstrate that P. succinatutens produces sufficient propionate in situ for this effect.
papers.modelSystem: Murine mono-colonization (L3); Porcine dietary fiber fermentation models (L2)
papers.keyLimitation: Strain-specific causal validation missing in pigs: propionate's barrier effect validated, but P. succinatutens' in situ propionate production capacity in porcine gut not demonstrated.
papers.summary: Best-validated probiotic function node. Dual-pathway propionate signaling mechanism fully characterized. Translational gap: strain-specific propionate production quantification in porcine gut lumen and epithelium needed to advance from L2 to L3 in pigs.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Lipid Deposition Reduction via MYC-Arginine-FOXO3a Axis
papers.abstract: P. succinatutens reduces host lipid deposition through the propionate-mediated MYC-arginine-FOXO3a molecular axis: HDAC inhibition leads to MYC transcriptional attenuation, derepression of glutamine transporters (SLC1A5/SLC38A5) enhances de novo arginine biosynthesis, and arginine accumulation suppresses PI3K/Akt/FOXO3a signaling to reduce adipogenic gene expression. Murine FMT and mono-colonization experiments have fully validated this pathway (Level 3). In porcine models, UCP1 knock-in pigs show negative correlation between fecal SCFAs and adipogenic gene expression (Level 1 association). The pathway is not P. succinatutens-specific in the porcine context. Functional divergence from P. faecium (which operates via TLR2) constitutes genus-level metabolic specialization.
papers.modelSystem: Murine FMT + mono-colonization (L3); Porcine UCP1 knock-in correlation (L1)
papers.keyLimitation: Pathway validated in mice only; porcine evidence is SCFA-adipogenic gene correlation, not P. succinatutens-specific. Pathway conservation across commercial pig breeds is speculative.
papers.summary: Demonstrates genus-level functional divergence (P. succinatutens vs P. faecium). MYC-arginine-FOXO3a axis is a potential target for lipid reduction in livestock. Porcine validation remains entirely at association level.

papers.title: Butyrate/SCFAs: Compartmentalized Co-Metabolism Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Butyrate is the most extensively validated node in the compartmentalized co-metabolism network. Forward pathway (microbial metabolites to host epigenetics): Level 2b in vivo validation in porcine models. Xiong et al. (2016) landmark study demonstrated butyrate upregulates endogenous host defense peptides via HDAC inhibition in weaned piglets. Porcine ileal organoid experiments confirmed HDAC inhibition and barrier-enhancing effects. Tibetan pig studies showed butyrate promotes macrophage H4K31 crotonylation via mTORC1-HDAC3/8. Reverse pathway (host epigenetics to microbiota): only Level 1. Host gene regulatory networks (PRDM15, STAT1) and germ-free pig transcriptome data provide hypothesis-generating clues. Coupling: Level 0. Forward and reverse effects never simultaneously observed in a single experimental system. Measurement Depth: Level 0. All studies measured only a single compartment (luminal) at a single timepoint. The three-order-of-magnitude concentration difference between luminal (mM) and intracellular (muM) butyrate has not been incorporated into experimental design.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, Tibetan pigs) + ileal organoids
papers.keyLimitation: Forward pathway at L2b but reverse pathway at L1 and coupling at L0. The fundamental butyrate concentration gradient (lumen mM vs epithelium muM) has never been incorporated into experimental design. No study has simultaneously measured forward and reverse effects.
papers.summary: Most-validated co-metabolism node. Forward pathway well-characterized at L2b in pigs (HDAC inhibition, host defense peptides, crotonylation). But reverse pathway, coupling verification, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0-L1. Represents the core evidence asymmetry in the co-metabolism framework.

papers.title: Bile Acids: Host-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Bile acids are a porcine-validated core node. Forward pathway: Level 2b. Hyodeoxycholic acid (HDCA), the dominant porcine bile acid, enhances intestinal barrier function via FXR-PI3K/AKT pathway in weaned piglets, increasing Lactobacillus abundance from 5.28% to 37.97%. Neonatal piglets show bile acid-mediated epithelial proliferation and barrier maturation through TGR5 activation and FXR downregulation. Substantial species differences in bile acid pool composition (pigs: HDCA-dominant; mice: CA/DCA-dominant) limit murine evidence extrapolation. Reverse pathway: only Level 1. Whether host bile acid signaling alterations feed back to regulate microbial community composition has not been causally validated. Coupling: Level 0. FXR/TGR5 activation and microbial restructuring never simultaneously tracked. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Main evidence gap: concentration gradient data for bile acids at the lumen-epithelium interface are absent.
papers.keyLimitation: Species-specific bile acid composition (HDCA-dominant in pigs) renders murine data non-directly translatable. Reverse pathway causal validation void is the critical bottleneck for understanding bile acid-microbiota feedback circuits.
papers.summary: Porcine-validated forward pathway with species-specific bile acid (HDCA). Lactobacillus expansion from 5.3% to 38.0% is the strongest microbial community shift documented in any node. Reverse pathway (host-to-microbiome bile acid signaling) is the critical evidence gap. FXR-dependent antimicrobial peptide pathway recently identified as potential reverse mechanism.

papers.title: Tryptophan Metabolites: AhR Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Tryptophan metabolites are partially porcine-validated. Forward pathway: Level 2a (preliminary in vivo). Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron alleviates weaning diarrhea in piglets via tryptophan metabolism-AhR-Nrf2 signaling. Gut microbiota-mediated tryptophan metabolism suppresses pyroptosis via AhR signaling to maintain intestinal epithelial homeostasis in piglets. Dietary tryptophan supplementation modulates gut microbial composition and activates AhR signaling. Validations are based on specific bacterial strains or dietary interventions, not direct causal validation of tryptophan metabolites per se. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Tryptophan metabolite reverse regulatory role completely unexplored. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gap: concentration gradients of tryptophan metabolites (indole, kynurenine) across lumen-epithelium-microenvironment compartments never simultaneously measured. Temporal asynchrony between AhR activation and microbial community shifts uncharacterized.
papers.keyLimitation: Validations are strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) or dietary, not direct metabolite causal validation. AhR activation and pyroptosis suppression not linked causally in porcine gut in vivo. Reverse pathway entirely unexplored.
papers.summary: AhR signaling is the central mechanism linking tryptophan metabolites to intestinal homeostasis. Strain-specific (B. thetaiotaomicron) and dietary intervention evidence at L2a. Direct metabolite causal validation, reverse pathway, and multi-compartment measurement all at L0.

papers.title: Polyamines: Cross-Species Extrapolation Node (Priority P3)
papers.abstract: Polyamines are a cross-species extrapolated predicted node with no direct validation evidence at any level in porcine models. Forward pathway: only Level 1. All existing evidence derives from murine models and in vitro studies: polyamines regulate histone methylation and acetylation by promoting nucleosome condensation; polyamines and gut microbiota form bidirectional interactions maintaining intestinal homeostasis (including microbial growth and biofilm formation). None of these mechanisms have been experimentally verified in the porcine gut. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: baseline concentration and spatial distribution data for polyamines in the porcine gut are entirely absent; polyamine biosynthetic capacity of porcine-derived strains uncharacterized; polyamine transporter expression patterns in porcine intestinal epithelium unknown.
papers.modelSystem: Murine models and in vitro only
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine data at any level. Baseline polyamine concentrations in porcine gut unknown. Porcine-derived polyamine-producing strains not isolated. Entirely dependent on cross-species extrapolation from murine evidence.
papers.summary: Weakest evidence base among all five nodes in porcine context. All evidence is murine-derived. Inclusion based on established mammalian host-microbe co-metabolic functions. Porcine functional relevance requires de novo establishment. First essential step: baseline polyamine concentration measurement in porcine gut compartments.

papers.title: Vitamin B12: One-Carbon Metabolism Node (Priority P3)
papers.abstract: Vitamin B12 has the weakest evidence base among all five nodes. Forward pathway: only Level 1 (association inference). B12 is a key cofactor in host one-carbon metabolism, participating in S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) synthesis to provide methyl donors for DNA methylation. This theoretical link is elaborated in reviews but direct causal validation between B12 concentrations and DNA methylation marks is absent in porcine models. In P. succinatutens' succinate-to-propionate pathway, B12 is the essential coenzyme for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. B12 availability directly impacts propionate production efficiency, representing the functional intersection with Project 1. Reverse pathway: Level 0. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Core evidence gaps: B12 concentration distribution across porcine gut compartments unknown; B12-propionate metabolic coupling never validated in situ in porcine gut; causal effect of B12 on porcine intestinal epithelial DNA methylation untested.
papers.modelSystem: Theoretical/review-level only; no direct experimental validation
papers.keyLimitation: Weakest of all five nodes. Entirely at theoretical/review level. No experimental data on B12-DNA methylation link in porcine gut. B12-propionate coupling in situ completely untested. P. succinatutens B12 dependence is the only functional anchor in the co-metabolism network.
papers.summary: Weakest evidence node but critical for understanding one-carbon metabolism integration with microbial SCFA production. Functional intersection with P. succinatutens (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase requires B12) provides the mechanistic rationale for inclusion. Entirely a hypothesis awaiting experimental testing in porcine models.

papers.title: Propionate: Succinate-to-Propionate Pathway Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Propionate is a major microbial SCFA in the porcine gut, produced via the succinate pathway (dominant in Bacteroidetes and Phascolarctobacterium) and the acrylate pathway (dominant in Firmicutes). Forward pathway (microbial propionate → host): Level 2b in pigs. Propionate activates GPR41/GPR43 in porcine intestinal epithelium, suppresses HDAC activity, and serves as a gluconeogenic substrate in the porcine liver. The P. succinatutens methylmalonyl-CoA pathway directly couples succinate consumption to propionate production, representing a B12-dependent metabolic intersection. Reverse pathway (host → microbial propionate): Level 1. Dietary fiber composition and host bile acid secretion patterns influence propionate-producing community structure, but direct host genetic control over propionate output is uncharacterized. Key evidence gap: propionate concentration gradients across the porcine gut epithelium have never been systematically mapped. Bidirectional coupling remains at Level 0.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine dietary intervention + in vitro bacterial cultures
papers.keyLimitation: Propionate concentration gradients across gut compartments unmapped. P. succinatutens in situ propionate production rate in porcine gut unknown. Reverse pathway entirely at association level. B12-propionate coupling never measured in vivo.
papers.summary: Propionate is the second-most abundant SCFA in the porcine hindgut and a core output of the succinate-to-propionate pathway. Wraps P. succinatutens metabolism (succinate→propionate), B12 cofactor requirement (methylmalonyl-CoA mutase), and host GPR41/43 signaling into a single integrated node. Forward pathway partially validated at L2b in pigs; reverse pathway and coupling entirely unexplored.
papers.forwardJustification: Propionate-GPR41/43 signaling validated in porcine epithelium. HDAC inhibition and gluconeogenic roles characterized. P. succinatutens pathway fully mapped in vitro.
papers.reverseJustification: No causal evidence for host control of propionate-producing community in pigs. Dietary fiber → propionate association established but no host genetic or immune mechanism characterized.

papers.title: Acetate: Cross-Feeding & Systemic Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Acetate is the most abundant SCFA in the porcine gut (typically 50-60% of total SCFAs) and the primary substrate for butyrate-producing bacteria via cross-feeding. Forward pathway (microbial acetate → host): Level 2a in pigs. Acetate crosses the gut epithelium via MCT1/SMCT1 transporters, enters the portal circulation, and reaches peripheral tissues at high concentrations (100-200 muM in human portal blood; porcine data limited). Acetate activates GPR43 on enteroendocrine L-cells, stimulates GLP-1/PYY secretion, and serves as a lipogenic substrate and epigenetic modifier (acetyl-CoA for histone acetylation). Reverse pathway (host → acetate producers): Level 1. Host diet composition and intestinal transit time influence acetogenic community structure, but host-driven feedback mechanisms are unexplored. Key evidence gap: the gut microbial acetate economy in pigs — production rates, cross-feeding fluxes, and epithelial transport kinetics — lacks systematic quantification.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine dietary fiber models (association level); Murine mono-colonization (causal)
papers.keyLimitation: Acetate cross-feeding network structure in porcine gut unknown. Portal acetate concentration data absent for pigs. Acetate contribution to host histone acetylation in porcine intestinal epithelium never quantified. Reverse pathway entirely at theoretical level.
papers.summary: Acetate is quantitatively dominant but mechanistically understudied in pigs. Its role as the primary cross-feeding substrate for butyrate producers makes it a critical upstream node in the SCFA network. Porcine-specific portal and peripheral acetate kinetics are a major data gap. Histone acetylation link offers a direct epigenetic readout for microbial acetate activity.
papers.forwardJustification: GPR43 activation and GLP-1/PYY secretion by acetate validated in murine models. Acetate-to-butyrate cross-feeding well-characterized in vitro. Porcine evidence limited to dietary fiber → acetate correlations.
papers.reverseJustification: No evidence for host-directed control of acetogenic community composition. Host diet as driver established; host immune/genetic feedback unexplored.

papers.title: Folate/B9: One-Carbon Metabolism & Methyl Donor Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Folate (vitamin B9) is a key microbial product in the gut that participates in host one-carbon metabolism alongside B12. Gut bacteria (especially Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus species) are net folate producers, contributing 10-30% of host folate requirements. Forward pathway (microbial folate → host): Level 1 in pigs. Microbial folate is absorbed in the colon via reduced folate carrier (RFC) and proton-coupled folate transporter (PCFT), contributing to host methyl donor pools (SAM cycle) and nucleotide synthesis. Bifidobacterium adolescentis folate production has been demonstrated in vitro and in murine models, but porcine in vivo evidence is absent. Reverse pathway (host → microbial folate): Level 1. Host dietary folate intake and the host folate absorption efficiency set the competitive landscape for microbial folate producers vs. auxotrophs. In pigs, dietary folic acid supplementation alters gut microbial composition but the mechanism (direct vs. indirect via host metabolism) is unclear. Coupling: Level 0. Measurement Depth: Level 0. Key gap: gut luminal folate concentrations and microbial folate production rates in pigs are completely unknown.
papers.modelSystem: Murine Bifidobacterium mono-association (L2a); Porcine dietary folic acid (L1 association)
papers.keyLimitation: Porcine gut luminal folate concentrations unknown. Microbial folate production rates in pig gut never measured. Bifidobacterium/Lactobacillus folate contribution to host methyl donor pool unquantified in any large animal model. B12-folate metabolic intersection in porcine gut unexplored.
papers.summary: Folate represents the microbial contribution to host one-carbon metabolism at the methyl donor level. Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus are the primary porcine-relevant folate producers. The B12-folate intersection in one-carbon metabolism is mechanistically established in human/murine systems but untested in pigs. Luminal concentration and production rate data are the prerequisite measurements.
papers.forwardJustification: Microbial folate production demonstrated in Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus in vitro. Colonic folate absorption via RFC/PCFT characterized in rodents. Porcine evidence: dietary folic acid changes gut microbiota composition at association level only.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary folate status influences competitive dynamics between folate producers and auxotrophs. Porcine dietary folic acid → microbiota shift observed but mechanistic pathway (direct microbial vs. host-mediated) not resolved.

papers.title: Vitamin A/Retinoic Acid: Immune-Microbiome Signaling Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Vitamin A and its active metabolite retinoic acid (RA) sit at the host-microbiome interface, regulating both host immunity and gut microbial ecology. Forward pathway (microbe-influenced RA → host): Level 2a in pigs. Gut bacteria influence host vitamin A metabolism through: (1) microbial production of retinaldehyde dehydrogenase (RALDH) cofactors that modulate host RA synthesis; (2) microbial SCFAs promoting dendritic cell RALDH expression and RA production. Porcine weaning stress decreases intestinal RALDH activity, correlating with microbial community disruption. Reverse pathway (host RA → microbiome): Level 2b in pigs. Retinoic acid directly regulates the gut microbiome through: (1) RA-induced epithelial IL-22/STAT3 signaling altering AMP secretion profiles; (2) RA-RAR signaling in B cells promoting IgA class switching, which shapes microbial composition; (3) RA modulation of Treg/Th17 balance influencing immune tolerance to commensals. This reverse pathway is better characterized than most nodes because the RA-RAR axis is well-studied. Coupling: Level 0 — RA effects on host immunity and microbial composition never tracked simultaneously in pigs. Measurement Depth: Level 0.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine weaning stress models (L2a); Murine RAR knockout + gnotobiotic (L3)
papers.keyLimitation: Simultaneous measurement of RA-dependent host immune activation AND microbial community shifts not performed in pigs. Porcine intestinal RA concentration and RALDH activity spatial mapping absent. The RA-RAR-IgA axis relevance to porcine gut commensal control unquantified.
papers.summary: Vitamin A/retinoic acid is the best-characterized REVERSE pathway node. The RA-RAR-IgA axis provides a mechanistically detailed host-to-microbiome signaling route. The forward pathway (microbes → host RA metabolism) is less characterized, creating an unusual reverse-dominant evidence asymmetry. Key AhR-RA crosstalk at the tryptophan-vitamin A metabolic intersection is unexplored in pigs.
papers.forwardJustification: Porcine weaning stress reduces intestinal RALDH activity (enzyme-level evidence). Microbial SCFA promotion of DC RALDH expression established in mice. Causal microbial → RA → host immune effect not demonstrated in pigs.
papers.reverseJustification: RA-RAR signaling in B cell IgA class switching well-characterized (molecular pathway). RA-IL-22-STAT3-AMP axis established. Porcine dietary vitamin A alters gut microbiota composition. Strongest reverse pathway evidence among all non-Bile Acid nodes at L2b.

papers.title: P. succinatutens: Porcine Gut Strain Isolation & Characterization Status
papers.abstract: Phascolarctobacterium succinatutens is an obligate succinate-consuming, propionate-producing bacterium in the family Acidaminococcaceae. It occupies a keystone metabolic niche in the porcine gut: its succinate-to-propionate pathway prevents luminal succinate accumulation (which would otherwise fuel Enterobacteriaceae blooms) while producing propionate for host signaling. Current isolation status: YIT 12066 and YIT 12067 were isolated from human feces (Yakult Central Institute, 2012). NO porcine-derived strain has been isolated to date. 16S rRNA gene surveys place Phascolarctobacterium at 1-5% relative abundance in healthy pig feces across diverse breeds (Yorkshire, Landrace, Duroc, Meishan). Metagenomic binning has recovered near-complete P. succinatutens genomes from pig fecal metagenomes. Core metabolic pathway (methylmalonyl-CoA) shows 95-100% amino acid sequence identity between human and porcine metagenome-assembled genomes. Key bottleneck: without a porcine-derived isolate, mono-colonization experiments in piglets cannot be performed, blocking L3→L4 translational advance.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine 16S/metagenomic surveys (L1); Human isolate in vitro (L2); Murine cross-colonization (L3)
papers.keyLimitation: No porcine-derived P. succinatutens isolate exists — the single most important bottleneck for advancing the translational pipeline from murine L3 to porcine L4. Without a pig-derived strain, causal validation in piglets is impossible.
papers.summary: P. succinatutens is the highest-priority strain for porcine isolation. Its succinate-to-propionate pathway is the mechanistic center of the co-metabolism framework. The human isolate (YIT 12066/12067) provides a genetic template, but porcine strain isolation is the prerequisite for advancing the entire Project 1 translational pipeline from L3 to L4. Core metabolic pathway is conserved but host-specific colonization factors remain unknown.
papers.forwardJustification: Succinate-to-propionate pathway fully characterized in human isolates. Methylmalonyl-CoA pathway enzymes (ScpA/ScpB/ScpC) 95-100% conserved in porcine MAGs. In vitro propionate production demonstrated. Host signaling effects inferred from propionate node.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary and immunological factors influencing P. succinatutens abundance in pig gut completely uncharacterized. Porcine intestinal factors driving niche specialization unknown. Dietary succinate availability is the only characterized host input.

papers.title: Lactobacillus: Porcine Gut Probiotic Ecology Node (Priority P1)
papers.abstract: Lactobacillus species (including Lactiplantibacillus, Limosilactobacillus, and Ligilactobacillus per reclassified taxonomy) are dominant small intestinal colonizers in pigs, reaching 10^7-10^9 CFU/g in the duodenum and jejunum. They are the most extensively studied porcine gut bacteria, with >200 published porcine feeding trials. Forward pathway (Lactobacillus → host): Level 2b in pigs. Multiple porcine trials demonstrate Lactobacillus-mediated pathogen inhibition (E. coli K88, Salmonella Typhimurium), intestinal barrier enhancement (ZO-1, occludin upregulation), and immune modulation (TLR2/TLR4 signaling, IgA promotion). However, strain-level specificity is high — effects observed with one strain (e.g., L. plantarum JDFM LP11) do not generalize. Reverse pathway (host → Lactobacillus): Level 2a in pigs. Host dietary fiber composition, weaning stress, and intestinal mucin glycosylation patterns all influence Lactobacillus community composition. Host antimicrobial peptide secretion (RegIII-gamma) selectively suppresses Lactobacillus in a porcine weaning model. Key gap: strain-level resolution is required — genus-level 16S data obscures functional divergence between species/strains.
papers.modelSystem: Porcine in vivo (weaned piglets, >200 trials) + porcine intestinal organoids
papers.keyLimitation: Genus-level data dominate; strain-level functional divergence is critical but under-characterized. Reclassified taxonomy (Lactobacillus → 25 genera, 2020) not reflected in most porcine literature. Strain-specific colonization factors and persistence in porcine gut poorly understood.
papers.summary: Lactobacillus is the most extensively studied porcine gut bacterium but suffers from nomenclatural fragmentation and inadequate strain-level resolution. Forward pathway evidence at L2b (barrier, immune, pathogen inhibition), reverse pathway at L2a (host AMPs and weaning stress). Represents the best-characterized bidirectional system among all strain nodes but lacks coupling (never measured simultaneously in same animals).
papers.forwardJustification: Extensive porcine trial literature: barrier enhancement (ZO-1/occludin), pathogen inhibition (E. coli, Salmonella), immune modulation (TLR2/4, IgA). Strain specificity high — effects not generalizable across Lactobacillus species.
papers.reverseJustification: Porcine weaning stress alters Lactobacillus community structure. Host RegIII-gamma AMP selectively suppresses Lactobacillus in piglets. Dietary fiber → Lactobacillus bloom established. Host mucin glycosylation influence demonstrated at L2a.

papers.title: Prevotella: Porcine Hindgut Dominance & Succinate Node (Priority P2)
papers.abstract: Prevotella is the dominant Gram-negative genus in the porcine hindgut, often representing 15-30% of the fecal metagenome — substantially higher than in humans. Prevotella species are major succinate producers in the pig colon, making them the primary upstream metabolic partner for succinate-consuming bacteria (P. succinatutens). Forward pathway (Prevotella → host): Level 2a in pigs. Prevotella-dominated enterotypes are associated with higher luminal SCFA concentrations and improved feed efficiency in pigs. Prevotella copri produces succinate as a fermentation end-product, which is then cross-fed to propionate producers. Specific Prevotella species encode carbohydrate-active enzymes (CAZymes) targeting porcine dietary fibers (arabinoxylan, pectin). Reverse pathway (host → Prevotella): Level 2a. Host dietary carbohydrate composition is the dominant driver of Prevotella abundance. Porcine host genotype (MUC4, FUT1 polymorphisms) influences Prevotella colonization. Key gap: Prevotella-to-P. succinatutens succinate cross-feeding has never been directly measured in the porcine gut in situ. The succinate intermediate pool size and flux rate are unknown.
papers.keyLimitation: Prevotella-to-Phascolarctobacterium succinate cross-feeding never measured in situ in porcine gut. Succinate intermediate pool size unknown. Prevotella species-level functional diversity in pigs poorly characterized — genus-level data mask substantial metabolic heterogeneity.
papers.summary: Prevotella is the dominant succinate producer and primary metabolic partner for P. succinatutens in the porcine hindgut. The Prevotella→succinate→Phascolarctobacterium→propionate cascade is the central metabolite flow in the co-metabolism framework. Host dietary fiber composition is the dominant top-down driver of Prevotella abundance. Porcine-specific Prevotella CAZyme repertoire and succinate production kinetics are key unknowns.
papers.forwardJustification: Prevotella-dominated enterotype → higher SCFA and improved feed efficiency in multiple porcine cohorts. P. copri succinate production characterized in vitro. Porcine Prevotella CAZyme diversity documented at metagenomic level. Direct succinate flux to propionate producers not measured.
papers.reverseJustification: Host dietary carbohydrate composition is the dominant driver — well-established in multiple porcine dietary intervention trials. Host genotype (MUC4, FUT1) influence documented. Weaning transition shifts Prevotella abundance, driven by dietary change.

papers.title: Butyrate ameliorates maternal high-fat-diet-induced placental inflammation and offspring metabolic dysfunction via modulating gut microbiota in mice.
papers.abstract: Maternal high-fat diet (HFD) increases the risk of metabolic disorders in offspring. Placental inflammation acts as a critical mediator with poorly addressed etiology. Recently HFD-induced gut dysbiosis is demonstrated to be a key driver of systemic inflammation. Whether inflammatory signals triggered by HFD-induced gut dysbiosis are transmitted to the placenta via the maternal-fetal axis warrant further investigation. This study aims to elucidate the mechanistic connection between maternal gut

papers.title: Cadmium toxicity to the human gut microbiome varies depending on composition.
papers.abstract: Cadmium (Cd(II), hereafter Cd) is a toxic heavy metal that has detrimental effects on the gut microbiota. We investigated the effects of acute Cd exposure on human fecal microbiotas using 24-h in vitro cultures from 20 healthy adult donors. Regression analysis of butyrate production in the absence (-Cd) versus presence (+Cd) of Cd identified three categories of microbial responses: sensitive, intermediate, and resilient. Under Cd stress, sensitive microbiomes exhibited significant decreases in b

papers.title: From Dysbiosis to Diabetes: How Gut Microbiome Interventions Influence Type 2 Diabetes.
papers.abstract: Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) is a complex metabolic disorder associated with insulin resistance (IR), chronic low-grade inflammation, and dysregulated glucose metabolism. Increasing evidence suggests that gut microbiota imbalances, or dysbiosis, may play a key role in its development and progression. This review aims to critically evaluate the existing literature on the role of gut microbiome-targeted interventions, specifically prebiotics and probiotics, in the prevention and management of T2D. The re

papers.title: Rhubarb extract ameliorates slow-transit constipation in rats by modulating gut microbiota and bile acid homeostasis.
papers.abstract: Rhubarb, the dried rhizome and root of Rheum species, is a traditional Chinese medicine widely used for constipation relief. However, the mechanisms underlying its therapeutic effects on slow-transit constipation (STC) remain unclear.

papers.title: Isolation and functional characterization of goose-derived lactic acid bacteria with growth-promoting and gut health benefits in Sanhua geese.
papers.abstract: Lactic acid bacteria (LAB) offer multiple probiotic benefits, yet host-specific strains for geese remain understudied. This study evaluated three goose-derived LAB strains, AK-GRW1, AK-GRW2, and AK-GRW3, for their effects on growth and intestinal health in geese. In vitro, AK-GRW1 exhibited the strongest antibacterial activity and acid production capacity, while AK-GRW2 showed the highest gastrointestinal tolerance. Additionally, all three strains remained sensitive to common antibiotics. In viv

papers.title: The milk fat globule membrane increases bacterial serotonin and exopolysaccharide production for maintenance of intestinal cell differentiation.
papers.abstract: Serotonin is a neurotransmitter that is primarily produced in the gut and is produced by lactic acid bacteria (LAB). It plays a crucial role in regulating mood and mental health disorders via the gut-brain axis using the enteric nervous system. Exopolysaccharides (EPS) are complex metabolic end products of probiotic LAB that contribute to health-promoting activities in the intestine. Interactions between LAB and components in the food matrix, such as the milk fat globule membrane (MFGM), may alt

papers.title: Towards ecological psychiatry: The herbicide glyphosate disrupts behavior through microbiota-gut-brain axis.
papers.abstract: Planetary and human health including mental health are closely interrelated. Increasing evidence also points to a role for the microbiota-gut-brain axis in maintaining optimum mental health. Emerging evidence raises concerns about the unintended toxicity of environmental exposure to xenobiotics, any substance foreign to the body, on the brain and behavior. Glyphosate is one of the most widely used active ingredients for herbicides for both agricultural and domestic applications. However, investi

papers.title: Intranasal stromal cell-derived factor-1α mitigates parkinsonian deficits via dual modulation of neuroinflammation and gut microbiota in MPTP-induced models.
papers.abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by dopaminergic neuronal loss and neuroinflammation, with emerging evidence implicating gut-brain axis dysregulation in its pathogenesis. Stromal cell-derived factor-1α (SDF-1α), a chemokine with neuroprotective properties, remains underexplored as a therapeutic agent for PD. This study investigates the efficacy of intranasal SDF-1α administration in mitigating motor deficits, gastrointestinal (GI) dysfunction, an

papers.title: The interplay between gut microbiota and Alzheimer's disease: mechanistic insights from dysbiosis to disease modulation.
papers.abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a chronic, progressive, neurodegenerative condition marked by memory loss and cognitive decline. It is characterized by neuropathological features such as amyloid plaque accumulation, neurofibrillary tangles of tau protein, and inflammatory changes in the brain. Recent research emphasizes how gut microbes influence the onset and progression of AD primarily through the gut-brain connection, a bidirectional communication system. The human gastrointestinal tract (GI) con

papers.title: Ketogenic diet alleviates acute radiation-induced intestinal injury through JAK2/STAT3/RORγt/IL-17A signaling pathway via gut microbiome.
papers.abstract: Emerging evidence suggests dietary interventions regulate inflammatory signaling through gut microbiome modulation, yet their therapeutic potential in radiation-induced intestinal injury (RIII) remains underexplored. This study demonstrates that ketogenic diet (KD), a high-fat and low-carbohydrate dietary regimen, exerts protective effects against RIII through dual mechanisms involving microbial regulation and inflammatory pathway inhibition. Using high-salt diet (HSD) as a dietary control, KD s

papers.title: Fasting primes small intestinal regeneration after damage via a microbiome-metabolite-chromatin axis.
papers.abstract: Fasting enhances small intestinal regeneration after radiation, but the contribution of the gut microbiome to this process remains uncharacterized. We identify Akkermansia muciniphila (AKK) as a key mediator of this response. AKK was enriched in fasted mice and its antibiotic depletion abrogated radioprotection, whereas reintroduction restored both organismal survival and intestinal integrity. Fasting elevated propionic acid, consistent with AKK's metabolic output. AKK-conditioned medium and pro
papers.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

papers.title: Abrus cantoniensis α-glucan-like polysaccharide alleviates influenza via gut microbial acetate to activate free fatty acid receptor 2/ mitochondrial antiviral signaling protein/interferon-beta pathway.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiota is critical for host defense against influenza. Polysaccharides are known for their microbiota-modulating and immunomodulatory activities; however, the anti-influenza efficacy of homogeneous Abrus cantoniensis polysaccharides (ACP) remains unexplored.
papers.journal: Phytomedicine : international journal of phytotherapy and phytopharmacology

papers.title: In situ sol-gel-sol transformation formed by sodium alginate realizes folic acid-modified chitosan nanoparticles to deliver hops β-acids for colorectal cancer therapy.
papers.abstract: Oral administration can deliver targeted nanoparticles directly to colorectal cancer (CRC) lesions, bypassing systemic circulation. However, orally administered nanoparticles may become physically entrapped in the small intestine, compromising therapeutic efficacy. To overcome this limitation, we designed and prepared folic acid-modified, CRC cell-targeting nanoparticles (FA/CS/TPP/β-acids) and incorporated them into a sodium alginate hydrogel (Alg/FA/CS/TPP/β-acids). The release of β-acids from

papers.title: A Common Indoor Pollutant, 1,4-Dichlorobenzene, Induces Colonic Inflammation via Vitamin D Receptor-Reg3γ-Gut Microbiota Axis.
papers.abstract: 1,4-Dichlorobenzene (1,4-DCB), a widely used household insect repellent and fungicide, has been epidemiologically linked to ulcerative colitis, yet its potential mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this study, juvenile male mice developed colonic inflammation following exposure to 1,4-DCB at doses of 0.03-3 mg/kg/day, a range corresponding to the human estimated daily intake (EDI) and relevant internal exposure levels in adolescents. This was evidenced by increased fecal moisture (by 12.22%-

papers.title: A Lactobacillus plantarum vaccine candidate with dendritic cell targeting and lysosome escape properities provides efficient protection against necrotic enteritis in broiler chickens.
papers.abstract: Necrotic enteritis (NE) caused by Clostridium perfringens (C.perfringen) type G strains remains a critical threat to the global poultry industry, particularly with the restricted use of antibiotics. The core virulence factor Necrotic Enteritis B-like Toxin (NetB), along with the adhesion factors Fimbrial protein A (FimA) and Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate aldolase (Fba), are key targets for multivalent vaccination. In this study, we developed a surface-displayed Lactobacillus plantarum (L. plantarum)

papers.title: Cichorium intybus L. polysaccharide improves growth performance and colonic barrier function in weaned piglets via the microbiota-HDCA-TGR5-Akt-NF-κB signaling axis: validation by FMT and in vitro models.
papers.abstract: Weaning stress predisposes piglets to intestinal barrier disruption and gut dysbiosis, which contribute to post-weaning diarrhea and poor feed efficiency. Chicory (Cichorium intybus L.) polysaccharide (CLP) is a fructan-rich prebiotic candidate; however, how CLP reshapes the microbiota-metabolite network to protect the colon remains unclear.
papers.journal: Journal of animal science and biotechnology

papers.title: Inhibition of the in vitro colonic fermentation of cooked gluten by dietary fibers with individual fermentability.
papers.abstract: Dietary fibers inhibit protein colonic fermentation, which could result from the availability, fermentability, and colloidal properties of dietary fibers, but the theory of dietary fibers acting on protein fermentation remains intricate. This paper reports the in vitro fermentation extent of steamed gluten supplemented with three dietary fibers of varying fermentability and colloidal properties. FOS inhibited gluten fermentation only at higher amounts, reducing the free amino acid (FAA) content

papers.title: Alleviating effects of 2'-fucosyllactose combined with Bifidobacterium longum BB536 on loperamide-induced constipation and depressive-like behavior.
papers.abstract: Constipation as a public health issue impairs human health and quality of life, while current strategies against constipation often lead to adverse effects. This study evaluated the effects and underlying mechanism of 2'-fucosyllactose (2'-FL) and Bifidobacterium longum BB536 (BB536) on relieving loperamide-induced constipation and depressive-like behavior. 2'-FL + BB536 can significantly elevate fecal water content, shorten defecation time and enhance the intestinal transit rate in constipated

papers.title: Lotus seed resistant starch alleviates OVA-induced food allergy in rats by promoting a Bifidobacterium-enriched gut microbiota and enhancing acetic acid production.
papers.abstract: This study established a rat model of ovalbumin (OVA)-induced food allergy. By systematically comparing allergic phenotypes, gut microbiota remodeling, and short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) profiles among groups receiving single interventions-Type 3 lotus seed resistant starch (LRS3), sodium acetate (AC), Bifidobacterium animalis subsp. lactis DSM 10140 (BA)-and combined interventions (LRS3-AC, LRS3-BA), a multi-level correlation network of "gut microbiota-SCFAs-immune markers" was constructed. Th

papers.title: Microbiota-gut-brain axis in autism spectrum disorder: integrating brain structure, function, and transcriptomics.
papers.abstract: Growing evidence indicates that disruption of the microbiota-gut-brain (MGB) axis is a key factor in autism spectrum disorder (ASD), affecting neurodevelopment, neural circuit function, and behavior. This review synthesizes multidisciplinary data to clarify mechanistic links between the MGB axis and ASD and to evaluate microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies. We conducted a narrative synthesis of clinical and translational studies, including human cohort and case-control investigations, anima

papers.title: Lactiplantibacillus plantarum SLpl116 attenuates OVA-induced food allergy with ecological restoration of the gut microbiota and immune rebalancing.
papers.abstract: Gut dysbiosis is increasingly recognized as a key contributor to food allergy, yet probiotic strains capable of restoring allergic microbiota and rebalancing host immunity remain limited. Here, we identified Lactiplantibacillus plantarum SLpl116 through a multi-criteria screening pipeline integrating anti-allergic activity, safety, and processing stability, and evaluated its efficacy in a prophylactic ovalbumin (OVA)-induced murine food allergy model. SLpl116 significantly attenuated allergic sy

papers.title: The Microbiome-Gut-Gonad Axis: How Microbial Metabolites Orchestrate Reproductive Physiology, Pathology, and Therapy.
papers.abstract: The human microbiome, a dynamic endocrine organ, exerts profound systemic influence through the production of bioactive metabolites. While the microbiome-gut-brain axis is well-established, the direct conduit between the gut microbiota and the reproductive system, the Microbiome-Gut-Gonad Axis, remains an emerging paradigm. This review explored cutting-edge evidence to construct a comprehensive model of the Microbiome-Gut-Gonad axis, focusing on the mechanistic roles of specific microbial metabo
papers.journal: The Journal of steroid biochemistry and molecular biology

papers.title: Limosilactobacillus reuteri Alleviates Parkinson's Disease by Regulating CDCA Secretion.
papers.abstract: To explore potential therapeutic agents for Parkinson's disease (PD), we investigated the impact of Limosilactobacillus reuteri (L. reuteri), a probiotic found to be significantly depleted in 6-hydroxydopamine (6-OHDA)-induced PD rat model. Despite its known benefits, the specific effects and underlying mechanisms of L. reuteri in PD remain poorly understood. In this study, we demonstrated that supplementation with L. reuteri alleviated motor deficits and attenuated dopamine (DA) neuron damage i

papers.title: Next Generation Probiotics Challenges: A Road to use as Live Bio-Therapeutics.
papers.abstract: There is an eminent association between human gut microbiota and health. The eventual footprint on the host depends heavily on the symbiotic relationship between the host and their gut microbiota. Current probiotics mostly available to consumers are drawn from a restricted arsenal of organisms, mostly belongs to various genera of Lactic acid bacteria (LAB). Ever expanding knowledge of gut microbiota and its related constituents is shifting this paradigm, specifically the phylogenetic range and t

papers.title: Paradigm Shift of Microbiota-gut-brain Axis During Aging: Potential Role of Probiotics to Improve Cognitive Decline.
papers.abstract: Population aging is a global demographic inevitability, driven by advancements in healthcare, increased life expectancy, and declining fertility rates. Growing evidence implicates gut microbiota dysbiosis in the pathogenesis of cognitive impairments and neurodegenerative disorders commonly associated with aging, primarily through disruptions in immune, metabolic, and neuroendocrine signaling along the gut-brain axis. This review synthesizes current literature on the therapeutic potential of prob

papers.title: Mucin degradation by Akkermansia muciniphila promotes Alistipes-dependent tryptophan metabolism and Th17-driven autoimmunity.
papers.abstract: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune disorder of the central nervous system associated with alterations in gut commensals, including Akkermansia muciniphila (A. muciniphila). However, its role in MS remains unclear. Here, we report elevated serum lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and anti-LPS IgG levels in patients with relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS), indicating compromised gut barrier integrity. Notably, RRMS patients also exhibited increased serum anti-A. muciniphila IgA and enhanced A. muciniphila-

papers.title: Optimal dietary nano-VD₃ enhances growth performance and bone development in broilers through remodeling the gut microbiome and metabolites.
papers.abstract: Skeletal maldevelopment is a significant challenge in broiler production, causing substantial economic losses. Vitamin D₃ (VD₃) plays a critical role in poultry skeletal health, but its optimal dietary inclusion level for medium-growth broilers remains to be determined due to bioavailability differences among forms. This study investigated the effects of dietary VD₃ levels (conventional vs. nano-formulated) on growth performance, bone development, and gut microbiota composition and metabolite pr

papers.title: Bifidobacterium longum subsp. infantis CCFM1269 alleviates neonatal maternal separation-induced colic in rats by regulating serotonin metabolism, the NGF/TrkA signaling pathway and gut microbiota.
papers.abstract: Infantile colic (IC), a common functional gastrointestinal disorder in early life, affects approximately 20% of infants worldwide and is associated with long-term health risks; nevertheless, effective interventions remain limited. This study investigated the alleviating effects and underlying mechanisms of Bifidobacterium longum subsp. infantis CCFM1269 on neonatal maternal separation (NMS)-induced IC-like symptoms in rats. The results showed that CCFM1269 intervention significantly increased th

papers.title: Combining sequence-based approaches with anaerobic microbiology and modelling to understand gut microbial communities.
papers.abstract: Gut micro-organisms possess biochemical capabilities that far exceed those of their mammalian hosts, particularly in the ability to gain energy from the breakdown of diet-derived plant material (fibre). This article reviews investigations into gut microbial communities conducted by Harry Flint and his research group. First, extracellular cellulosome and amylosome enzyme complexes were found to mediate the breakdown of plant cell walls and resistant starch by specialised Firmicutes bacteria, both
papers.journal: The Proceedings of the Nutrition Society

papers.title: Digestion and fermentation characteristics of the polysaccharides from Polygalae Radix processed by Glycyrrhizae Radix et Rhizoma and its effects on gut microbiota and metabolites.
papers.abstract: Polygalae Radix Processed by Glycyrrhizae Radix et Rhizoma (GPR) contains polysaccharides (PGPR) as key active ingredients, yet their oral digestive fate and gut microbiota interactions remain elusive. In this study, we employed in vitro digestion and fermentation models to investigate PGPR's structural stability, fermentation properties, and modulatory effects on gut microbial communities and metabolites. Digestion experiments revealed no significant alterations in PGPR's molecular weight, free

papers.title: Hyocholic acids: Third bile acids for neonatal health.
papers.abstract: Hyocholic acids (HCAs), synthesized by fetal CYP3A7, dominate the neonatal bile acid pool. By promoting regulatory T cells and suppressing T helper 17 cells, HCAs establish intestinal immune tolerance, guide healthy microbiota assembly, and protect infants from infections and gastrointestinal disorders (Zheng et al.).

papers.title: Streptococcus thermophilus 1131 and Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus 2038 induce immune responses via M cell-mediated transcytosis in an in vitro human gut model.
papers.abstract: This study aimed to elucidate in vitro how orally administered Streptococcus thermophilus 1131 and Lactobacillus delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus 2038, stimulated immune cells in the gut lamina propria and thereby enhanced the intestinal barrier function, as reported in our previous study.

papers.title: Structural characterization of a branched α-glucan from Pseudostellaria heterophylla and its neuroprotective effect against Alzheimer's disease via regulating the microbiota-gut-brain axis.
papers.abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder with limited therapeutic options. Emerging evidence highlights the gut-brain axis as a promising target, and natural polysaccharides offer multi-target advantages. Here, we isolated and structurally characterized PF30-3, a homogeneous branched α-D-glucan (69.91 kDa) from Pseudostellaria heterophylla, featuring a (1 → 4)-α-d-glucopyranosyl backbone with (1 → 4,6)-α-d-glucopyranosyl branching points. In LPS-stimulated RAW264.7 ma

papers.title: Ligilactobacillus salivarius Lbs57 Exerted Antibacterial Activity and Modulated Cecal Microbiota to Reduce Foodborne Pathogen Campylobacter jejuni.
papers.abstract: Campylobacter jejuni (C. jejuni) remains a major global foodborne pathogen, with poultry serving as a primary reservoir. This study evaluates the probiotic potential of Ligilactobacillus salivarius Lbs57 against foodborne pathogen C. jejuni. In vitro, the L. salivarius Lbs57 cell-free supernatant (CFS) exhibited broad-spectrum, thermostable antibacterial activity, with the strongest inhibition against C. jejuni. Characterization revealed the activity was pH and protease-sensitive, and resistant

papers.title: Akkermansia muciniphila-derived postbiotics reprogram immune balance to combat sepsis via the IDO1/Kyn/AhR metabolic axis.
papers.abstract: Sepsis is a life-threatening syndrome of organ dysfunction driven by a dysregulated immune response. Effective therapeutic strategies to restore immune homeostasis remain limited. Hypoacylated lipooligosaccharides (ALOS) from Akkermansia muciniphila have emerged as potential immunomodulatory postbiotics, yet their therapeutic potential in sepsis and the underlying mechanisms remain unexplored.

papers.title: Synbiotic alginate microcapsules incorporating a date seed polysaccharide-stabilized Pickering emulsion improve probiotic gastrointestinal survival and modulate SCFA production, gut microbiota, and fecal metabolome.
papers.abstract: This study formulated alginate microbeads incorporating a date seed polysaccharide-stabilized pickering emulsion (Alg:PSE) aimed at enhancing probiotic survival and regulating gut microbiota in a strain-dependent manner. Lacticaseibacillus rhamnosus and Bifidobacterium longum subsp. longum strains were individually encapsulated within alginate beads, alginate with the ultrasound-extracted date seed polysaccharide (UPS), and alginate with the UPS-stabilized pickering emulsion (PSE). Among various

papers.title: 6PPD-Q exposure disrupts intestinal homeostasis and impairs skeletal muscle function in mice.
papers.abstract: 6PPD-Q is an emerging tire-derived contaminant, but its mammalian toxicological mechanisms and inter-organ effects remain poorly understood. Because oral exposure directly interfaces with the intestine, we investigated whether 6PPD-Q disrupts intestinal homeostasis and is associated with skeletal muscle dysfunction through a microbiota-related gut-muscle axis, and whether post-exposure sodium butyrate (NaB) intervention can alleviate these alterations. Male C57BL/6 mice were orally exposed to 6P

papers.title: Psychological stress drives aging-like hematopoietic stem cell dysfunction through a brain-gut-bone marrow axis.
papers.abstract: Chronic stress influences hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs). However, how psychological stress regulates HSC function remains incompletely understood. Here, we show that psychological stress impairs HSC self-renewal and lymphoid differentiation, inducing aging-like phenotypes. Stress suppresses neuronal activity in the medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) and periaqueductal gray (PAG), leading to HSC dysfunction, whereas chemogenetic activation of these regions restores HSC function. Psychological stre

papers.title: [Live-combined Bifidobacterium, Lactobacillus, and Enterococcus improves heat tolerance and attenuates systemic inflammatory injury in heat-stroke rats via modulation of gut microbiota and intestinal barrier].
papers.abstract: To investigate the ameliorative effects of live-combined Bifidobacterium, Lactobacillus, and Enterococcus (LCBLE) on heat tolerance and systemic inflammatory injury in rats with heat stroke, and to explore its underlying mechanisms.
papers.journal: Zhejiang da xue xue bao. Yi xue ban = Journal of Zhejiang University. Medical sciences

papers.title: From economic crop to high-value product: Fermentation of chestnut protein by Lactobacillus rhamnosus ameliorates chronic inflammation via the gut microbiota-SCFAs axis.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the anti-inflammatory effect and mechanism of fermented chestnut protein (FCP), produced by Lactobacillus rhamnosus ML1-2, on high-fat diet (HFD) and lipopolysaccharide (LPS)-induced chronic inflammation in mice. The results showed that lactic acid bacteria (LAB) fermentation significantly promoted the degradation of macromolecular proteins in chestnut protein, and the release of abundant bioactive peptides, amino acids, and the functional component γ-aminobutyric acid (G

papers.title: Of mice and men-The emerging oral-gut-brain axis of health and disease.
papers.abstract: Oral health's inextricable links to systemic health are highlighted by the emerging oral-gut-brain axis and other well-known axes. There is growing evidence of a complex oral-gut-brain axis linking mouth and gut microbiomes with the central nervous system. Axis disruptions, characterized as oral and gut dysbiosis or microbial imbalances, can trigger oral and systemic inflammation and neuroinflammation, contributing to diseases such as Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease.

papers.title: Effect of Probiotic Supplementation in Older Individuals with Mild Cognitive Impairment and Alzheimer's Disease: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled, Triple-Blind Clinical Trial.
papers.abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common type of dementia in older adults and often precedes mild cognitive impairment (MCI). These conditions are associated with biological alterations involving chronic inflammation, neuronal dysfunction, and genomic instability. In parallel, growing evidence has suggested a role for the gut-brain axis in cognitive aging, and probiotics have been investigated as a potential strategy to modulate inflammatory and neurotrophic pathways. This randomized, triple-

papers.title: Role of Toll-like receptors and oral-gut-brain axis in neurodegenerative and neuropsychiatric disorders.
papers.abstract: The oral-gut-brain axis is a path connecting the gastrointestinal tract and the central nervous system (CNS). The gut microbiota influences the immune system, metabolism, and nerve cells through the production of neurotransmitters and microbial metabolites that can cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB). The interplay between neuroinflammation and altered oral and gut microbiota is a bidirectional complex path modulated by inflammatory mediators. Recent studies suggest a potential role for Toll-lik

papers.title: Blueberry anthocyanin-pretreated Akkermansia muciniphila enhances probiotic efficacy against metabolic syndrome via gut microbiota remodeling and hepatic signaling modulation.
papers.abstract: Targeting the gut microbiota is a promising strategy for treating metabolic syndrome (MetS). Previous studies have shown that dietary components, including polyphenols and dietary fibers, can modulate the abundance of Akkermansia muciniphila (AKK). However, whether dietary compounds can enhance the probiotic function of AKK through a pretreatment strategy remains largely unexplored. Here, we revealed a bidirectional interaction between blueberry anthocyanins (BA) and AKK, and demonstrated that B

papers.title: Effects of health-functional products on gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids production: an ex vivo and pilot human study.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the effects of health-functional products on short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) production using an ex vivo gut simulation platform alongside a human pilot study. The research proceeded in three phases: screening of 11 commercially available products (e.g., omega-3, vitamins/minerals, plant protein, and dietary fiber products) with fecal samples from 15 healthy individuals, validation of four candidates with 30 participants, and a two-month pilot trial with 32 subjects consu

papers.title: Investigating the Effect of Ginger-Derived Nanovesicles on the Growth and Metabolic Activity of Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron: An Isothermal Microcalorimetric Study.
papers.abstract: Plant-derived extracellular vesicles (EVs) have shown numerous health benefits, including modulation of the human gut microbiota. Herein, we employed isothermal microcalorimetry (IMC) to explore the effects of ginger-derived nanovesicles (G-NVs) on the growth and metabolic activity of Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron (Bt), a dominant saccharolytic commensal with promising next-generation probiotic potential. Bt was exposed to either G-NVs or the ginger extract separated from G-NVs (G-CTL) in defined

papers.title: Gut commensal Bacteroides-derived pantothenic acid alleviates metabolic syndrome.
papers.abstract: Pantothenic acid (PA), or vitamin B5, can be synthesized by gut commensals, but the contribution of microbial PA to metabolic health remains unclear. Here, we find that microbial PA supply is reduced in individuals with metabolic syndrome (MetS) and is associated with impaired gut barrier function and disease severity. Tracing microbial PA identifies Bacteroides fragilis as a key contributor, with panC required for PA biosynthesis, as confirmed by isotope tracing, bacterial culture, and germ-fre

papers.title: Oligosaccharide prebiotics in functional foods and therapeutics: innovations and challenges.
papers.abstract: Oligosaccharide prebiotics, such as inulin, fructooligosaccharides (FOS), and galactooligosaccharides (GOS), have demonstrated significant effects on gut microbiota and host health across in vitro, animal, and clinical studies. These studies consistently report an increase in beneficial bacteria, particularly Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus, leading to higher production of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) such as acetate, propionate, and butyrate. These metabolic changes are linked to improved

papers.title: Microbiome-mediated pharmacology of ginseng: Mechanistic insights into metabolic regulation and therapeutic potential.
papers.abstract: Ginseng, a traditional medicinal herb with a favorable safety profile, has long been used to promote systemic health. Recent studies reveal that many of its beneficial effects are mediated through interactions with the gut microbiota. Microbial enzymes convert parent ginsenosides into more absorbable and bioactive metabolites such as compound K, while ginseng reciprocally remodels the microbial community and metabolite composition by promoting the growth of beneficial taxa including Akkermansia,

papers.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Fatty Liver Disease: From Pathogenesis to Treatment.
papers.abstract: Metabolic dysfunction-associated fatty liver disease (MAFLD) has become the most prevalent chronic liver disease worldwide and represents a major hepatic manifestation of systemic metabolic dysfunction. The disease is closely linked to obesity and insulin resistance and progresses from simple hepatic steatosis to metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. Increasing evidence indicates that MAFLD pathogenesis involves complex interactions

papers.title: Tobacco smoking disrupts bile acid and tryptophan metabolism in multiple sclerosis.
papers.abstract: Smokers with multiple sclerosis (MS) experience worse disease, yet underlying mechanisms remain unknown. Smoking disrupts bile acid and tryptophan metabolism in non-MS populations; both pathways involve host-microbiome co-metabolism and have been linked to MS.

papers.title: Small Intestinal Bacterial Overgrowth: Microbiome Dysregulation, Gut-Brain Axis Disruption, and Systemic Consequences.
papers.abstract: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO) is a gastrointestinal disorder characterized by excessive bacterial colonization in the small intestine, leading to impaired digestion and nutrient malabsorption. Increasing evidence indicates that SIBO exerts systemic effects beyond the gut, contributing to metabolic, neurological, cardiovascular, dermatological, and autoimmune conditions. Current management strategies include nonsystemic antibiotics such as rifaximin, dietary interventions (low-FODM

papers.title: Genotoxic effects of 6PPD-quinone on juvenile European seabass (Dicentrarchus labrax): Impacts on DNA replication fidelity and oxidative DNA damage.
papers.abstract: Juvenile European seabass (Dicentrarchus labrax) were exposed to environmentally relevant 6PPD-quinone (2.85 µg/L) for five days to evaluate transcriptional, oxidative, and antioxidant responses and histopathology. The liver showed the strongest gene induction: cdc28 (∼75×), bax (48×), spc25 (45×), and hsp70 (42.5×). Kidney responses were moderate, with bax up ∼5.5× and hsp70 and casp9 ∼2×, while pcna, cenpi, cenpf, and cdc28 were downregulated (0.32-0.66×). Spleen and intestine exhibited marked

papers.title: The gut-bone axis in osteoporosis: Microbiota-associated immune-metabolic remodeling of the bone marrow microenvironment.
papers.abstract: Osteoporosis is a common systemic skeletal disease in the aging population, and its pathogenesis involves multiple mechanisms such as estrogen deficiency, aging, reduced mechanical load, chronic low-grade inflammation, and imbalance of the calcium‑phosphorus-vitamin D-PTH/FGF23 axis. In recent years, the gut-bone axis has increasingly been recognized as a complementary regulatory network for understanding the systemic heterogeneity of osteoporosis, rather than a single dominant pathway independe

papers.title: Microbiota metabolite lithocholic acid in cancer: Mechanisms and therapeutic potential.
papers.abstract: The human gut microbiome is centrally implicated in host disease and health control via the production of a range of bioactive metabolites. Of these, lithocholic acid (LCA), a derivative of microbial metabolism secondary bile acid, has received attention owing to its multifaceted functions in cancer. LCA, a hydrophobic microbiota-derived bile acid, was implicated in carcinogenicity and hepatotoxicity even in the ancient era. In this sense, studies have found a contrasting role of LCA as a tumor

papers.title: Postbiotics From Ligilactobacillus salivarius Enhance Mitochondrial Robustness Via TLR2 Signaling in Intestinal Macrophages.
papers.abstract: Postbiotics, defined as preparations of inanimate microorganisms and/or their components, have attracted increasing attention because of their stability and functional benefits. Although lactic acid bacteria (LAB) modulate immune responses and oxidative stress, their direct effects on host mitochondrial homeostasis remain poorly understood. In this study, we investigated whether postbiotics derived from Ligilactobacillus salivarius enhance mitochondrial robustness in porcine intestinal macrophag

papers.title: A novel paraprobiotic strain, Lentilactobacillus hilgardii H-50 enhances intestinal IgA production via TLR2-dependent IL-6 induction driven by its cell surface components.
papers.abstract: Lactic acid bacteria enhance intestinal immunoglobulin A (IgA) secretion; however, the underlying mechanisms are often strain specific. In this study, we found a novel strain, Lentilactobacillus hilgardii H-50, that enhanced intestinal IgA production among L. hilgardii strains. Heat-killed H-50 strain increased IgA secretion from mouse Peyer's patch cells and elevated faecal IgA levels in vivo. Although this strain also induced the secretion of interleukin-6 (IL-6) and IL-10, IL-6 was essential

papers.title: Galactose-functionalized engineered outer membrane vesicles for targeted delivery of enterocin Gr17 ameliorate Salmonella Typhimurium-induced colitis.
papers.abstract: Bacteriocins, antimicrobial peptides from lactic acid bacteria (LAB), represent promising agents against Salmonella Typhimurium-induced colitis. However, their proteinaceous nature leads to poor bioavailability and insufficient concentrations at infection sites, which significantly limits their application potential. Enterocin Gr17 exhibits potent antibacterial activity against S. typhimurium and effectively ameliorates pathogen-induced intestinal barrier damage. Herein, we innovatively develope

papers.title: Hesperidin as an Emerging Nutraceutical in Modern Health and Preventive Medicine: A Narrative Review.
papers.abstract: Hesperidin is a bioactive flavonoid found in citrus fruits and has become a promising nutraceutical with multidimensional health benefits, such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidative, cardio-, neuroprotective, and metabolic-regulatory. However, poor bioavailability, variations in clinical efficacy, and limited standardized formulations are some of the challenges confronting this bioactive compound. Over the years, nanotechnology has remained a source of great developments in improving the absorptio

papers.title: Bifidobacterium bifidum TMC3115-RAW264.7 cell conditioned mediums promote the synaptic development of primary hippocampal neuron via activating IL-6/JAK2/STAT3 signaling pathway.
papers.abstract: Immunomodulation represents a key mechanism through which the microbiota-gut-brain axis exerts its effects. While probiotics can promote central nervous system (CNS) development via this axis, current in vitro models hard to recapitulate the process whereby probiotics activate local immunity and subsequently deliver immune mediators to the CNS through systemic circulation. To address this gap, we established a probiotic-RAW264.7 cell-primary hippocampal neuron system for investigation of how liv

papers.title: Regulatory Effects of Functional Food Biscuits on Mouse Brain Function and Gut Microbiota.
papers.abstract: The concept of food-medicine homology, derived from traditional Chinese dietary culture, endows functional food biscuits with both nutritional value and therapeutic potency. Such biscuits are abundant in bioactive compounds including polysaccharides, dietary fiber, and flavonoids, which may exert regulatory effects through the microbiota-gut-brain axis (MGBA). This study explored the impacts of functional biscuits formulated with Gastrodia elata Bl., Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf, Coix lacryma-jobi L

papers.title: Ammonia in the crosshairs: microbial targets for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis prevention.
papers.abstract: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) is increasingly linked to disruptions of the gut/liver axis, yet the microbial mechanisms driving disease progression remain incompletely defined. Here, Qu et al. have identified ileal microbial ammonia production by Clostridium perfringens as a mechanistic driver of epithelial barrier dysfunction and hepatic CD8+ T cell remodeling in MASH. In nonhuman primate and mouse models of MASH, the authors demonstrated that the glycine-based tripept

papers.title: Dietary fat alters goblet cell function and microbial bile acid metabolism to promote intestinal lipid absorption in mice.
papers.abstract: Dietary fat reshapes host-microbiota interactions, yet the upstream events that mediate overnutrition-driven microbiome alterations and metabolic dysfunction remain unclear. Here we compared mouse models of diet-induced and genetic obesity using multi-omics to identify the colonic mucus niche as an early, diet-sensitive driver of metabolic dysfunction. Excessive dietary lipids impaired glutamine metabolism and redox homeostasis in goblet cells, thinning the mucus layer and depleting the mucus-ad

papers.title: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis exacerbated by Clostridium perfringens-derived ammonia is attenuated by tripeptide DT-109.
papers.abstract: The global prevalence of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) is rising, driven by a complex interplay of metabolic disturbances, inflammation, and fibrosis, yet effective treatment options remain limited. This study examined the relationships among intestinal microbial dysbiosis, ammonia production, and hepatic CD8+ T cell activity in MASH, then assessed the therapeutic potential of DT-109, a glycine-based tripeptide. We investigated the gut/liver axis across human cohorts an

papers.title: Vancomycin-resistant gut commensal Clostridium innocuum emerges as a pathobiont through synergy with toxigenic Clostridioides difficile.
papers.abstract: Clostridium innocuum is increasingly detected in patients with Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI), particularly following vancomycin treatment, and is associated with delayed clinical recovery and poor outcomes. Although C. innocuum lacks classical toxins, its lipopolysaccharide-like cytotoxic activity and emerging pathogenic role remain poorly understood. In this study, we conducted co-infection models to explore the interactions between toxigenic C. difficile and C. innocuum in human int

papers.title: Comparative analysis of the gut microbiota and metabolome between healthy and diarrheic bean geese (Anser fabalis) wintering at Caizi Lake, China.
papers.abstract: Diarrhea is common in wintering migratory waterbirds and is increasingly linked to alterations in gut microbiota and the metabolome. This study compared the gut microbial communities and metabolic profiles of diarrheic and healthy bean geese (Anser fabalis) wintering at Caizi Lake, China, using 16S rRNA amplicon sequencing and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). Diarrheic samples exhibited significantly elevated water content and distinct gut microbial community structure, with clea

papers.title: Dietary supplementation with Bacillus velezensis enhances the intestinal health of broiler chickens challenged with necrotic enteritis via reshaping the structure and function of the intestinal flora.
papers.abstract: Probiotics have great potential in maintaining intestinal health and ensuring the growth performance of broiler chickens, while the effects of Bacillus velezensis (BV) have not yet been fully characterized. In the present study, a necrotic enteritis model was established based on the combined infection of coccidia and Clostridium perfringens (CCP), and the effects of dietary supplementation with BV on growth performance and intestinal health were investigated. Our outcomes showed that dietary su

papers.title: Multi-omics reveals goat milk improves glucose homeostasis in type 2 diabetes mellitus mice by gut-liver axis.
papers.abstract: Emerging evidence suggests that goat milk exerts beneficial effects on insulin levels, insulin sensitivity, and glucose homeostasis in type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM). However, the underlying mechanisms need further investigation. Here, the metabolic profiles of goat milk, goat milk powder, and cow milk were compared, revealing distinct compositional signatures. In a high-fat diet/streptozotocin-induced T2DM mouse model, goat milk intervention significantly improved glucose homeostasis (fasting

papers.title: Multi-omics analysis of saccharomyces boulardii supplementation reveals coordinated microbiome, metabolic, and immune signaling changes accompanying tumor suppression.
papers.abstract: The gut microbiome shapes cancer progression and treatment responses, yet scalable microbiome-targeted interventions remain limited. We screened commercial probiotics for activation of the host aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) and identified the yeast Saccharomyces boulardii as a consistent AhR activator. In an immunocompetent syngeneic colorectal cancer model, daily oral gavage of S. boulardii slowed growth of established subcutaneous tumors without detectable tumor colonization. Integrated prof

papers.title: Perinatal ampicillin exposure alters murine maternal fecal bile acid and acylcarnitine profiles.
papers.abstract: Maternal intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP) and postpartum maternal antibiotic usage are increasingly common and have been linked to altered growth and immune development in offspring. However, the mechanisms underlying these effects, particularly those arising from indirect early-life exposure to antibiotics, remain poorly understood. Here, using a preclinical murine model, we examined the impact of in vivo antepartum and postpartum maternal ampicillin administration on the maternal fecal

papers.title: Parabacteroides distasonis alleviates Clostridioides difficile infection in mice while modulating secondary bile acids.
papers.abstract: Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) is a major cause of antibiotic-associated diarrhea, with frequent recurrences closely linked to antibiotic-induced dysbiosis of the gut microbiota and bile acid metabolism. Parabacteroides distasonis, a potential probiotic capable of converting primary to secondary bile acids, has shown therapeutic promise in several metabolic and inflammatory diseases. This study evaluated the preventive and therapeutic effects of P. distasonis against CDI and explored t

papers.title: Effect of Shenling Baizhu powder on diarrhea induced by fatigue and high-fat diet: association with gut microbiota and bile acid metabolism.
papers.abstract: This study evaluated the protective effect of Shenling Baizhu Powder (SLBZP) against diarrhea induced by fatigue combined with a high-fat diet and explored the potential associations with changes in gut microbiota, bile acid metabolism, intestinal barrier function, and inflammatory responses. A mouse model of diarrhea was established by continuous standing on a multiple-platform apparatus 4 h/day for 14 days, combined with twice-daily lard gavage during days 8-14, followed by gavage with SLBZP d

papers.title: Maternal cafeteria diet and methyl donor supplementation modulate gut microbiota and anxiety-like behavior in male offspring.
papers.abstract: Maternal cafeteria diet and methyl donor supplementation modulate gut microbiota and behavioral outcomes in male offspring.

papers.title: Microbiome-derived bile acids contribute to elevated antigenic response and bone erosion in rheumatoid arthritis
papers.abstract: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic, disabling and incurable autoimmune disease. It has been widely recognized that gut microbial dysbiosis is an important contributor to the pathogenesis of RA, although distinct alterations in microbiota have been associated with this disease. Yet, the metabolites that mediate the impacts of the gut microbiome on RA are less well understood. Here, with microbial profiling and non-targeted metabolomics, we revealed profound yet diverse perturbation of the gut

papers.title: Chemotactic responses of Trypanosoma brucei procyclic forms to proline and other metabolites
papers.abstract: Many protozoan parasites have complex life cycles entailing migration through different organs in their hosts, but the cues guiding them remain poorly understood. Using a semi-solid plate motility assay, we show that early procyclic forms of Trypanosoma brucei, the first stage to develop in the tsetse fly midgut, perceive several metabolites - including glucose, glycerol and proline - as chemoattractants, while the glycolytic end-product succinate acts as a repellent. During adaptation in the fl

papers.title: Integrated multi-omics profiling of gut bacterial extracellular vesicles links cargo composition to host transcriptional responses
papers.abstract: Bacterial extracellular vesicles (bEVs) enable gut microbiota to deliver bioactive cargo to host cells, yet the specific bacterial producers have not been systematically identified. Here, we profiled stool-derived bEVs from healthy individuals using metaproteomic profiling, revealing that vesiculation is widespread across gut bacterial phyla. We selected a subset of vesiculating species, showing that bEVs localize to distinct tissues in vivo, including extraintestinal sites such as lung, liver,

papers.title: Human alpha-defensin 5 stabilizes the enterovirus A71 capsid and blocks infection
papers.abstract: Human alpha-defensins are antimicrobial peptides abundantly expressed in neutrophils and the small intestine. They block infection of several families of non-enveloped DNA viruses by binding to and stabilizing the viral capsid during entry, thereby preventing the genome from reaching the nucleus to initiate replication. It is unclear if a similar mechanism also applies to RNA viruses. To study this further, we investigated the interaction of human alpha-defensin 5 (HD5) with enterovirus A71 (EV-
